title: GDPR Third-Party Processor Compliance Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28 (Processor obligations) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.15: GDPR Third-Party Processor Compliance Monitoring.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.15' running on CIM Network_Traffic data model, proxy/firewall logs, gdpr_processor_register.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: CIM Network_Traffic data model, proxy/firewall logs, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where All_Traffic.action="allowed"
  by All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_processor_register.csv dest_domain AS dest OUTPUT processor_name, dpa_signed, last_audit_date, approved_data_categories, sub_processors
| where isnotnull(processor_name)
| eval days_since_audit=if(isnotnull(last_audit_date), round((now()-strptime(last_audit_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0), 9999)
| eval audit_status=case(days_since_audit > 365, "OVERDUE", days_since_audit > 270, "DUE_SOON", 1=1, "CURRENT")
| eval bytes_gb=round(bytes_out/1073741824, 2)
| sort - bytes_gb
| table dest, processor_name, dpa_signed, bytes_gb, sources, audit_status, days_since_audit, sub_processors
description: Article 28 requires controllers to use only processors providing sufficient guarantees and to monitor their compliance. This use case tracks data flows to processors, monitors their security posture indicators, detects data transfers exceeding agreed scope, and flags processors with overdue security assessments — providing continuous evidence that processor oversight is active, not just contractual.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build `gdpr_processor_register.csv` from your Art. 28 processor inventory with destination domains/IPs, DPA status, and audit dates; (2) monitor actual data transfer volumes to each processor; (3) alert on processors with overdue audits or unsigned DPAs; (4) detect unexpected volume spikes indicating scope creep; (5) track sub-processor changes reported by primary processors.
visualization: Table (processor compliance status), Bar chart (data transfer by processor), Single value (overdue audits count), Timeline (transfer volume trends). Key fields: `audit_status`, `bytes_gb`, `days_since_audit`, `dest`, `dpa_signed`, `processor_name`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Transfers logged during pre-approved vendor agreements, standard contractual clauses in flight, or planned data migration windows.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Network_Traffic data model, proxy/firewall logs, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Build `gdpr_processor_register.csv` from your Art. 28 processor inventory with destination domains/IPs, DPA status, and audit dates; (2) monitor actual data transfer volumes to each processor; (3) alert on processors with overdue audits or unsigned DPAs; (4) detect unexpected volume spikes indicating scope creep; (5) track sub-processor changes reported by primary processors.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` sum(All_Traffic.bytes_out) as bytes_out dc(All_Traffic.src) as sources
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where All_Traffic.action="allowed"
  by All_Traffic.dest
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| lookup gdpr_processor_register.csv dest_domain AS dest OUTPUT processor_name, dpa_signed, last_audit_date, approved_data_categories, sub_processors
| where isnotnull(processor_name)
| eval days_since_audit=if(isnotnull(last_audit_date), round((now()-strptime(last_audit_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0), 9999)
| eval audit_status=case(days_since_audit > 365, "OVERDUE", days_since_audit > 270, "DUE_SOON", 1=1, "CURRENT")
| eval bytes_gb=round(bytes_out/1073741824, 2)
| sort - bytes_gb
| table dest, processor_name, dpa_signed, bytes_gb, sources, audit_status, days_since_audit, sub_processors
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Third-Party Processor Compliance Monitoring (Art. 28)** — Article 28 requires controllers to use only processors providing sufficient guarantees and to monitor their compliance. This use case tracks data flows to processors, monitors their security posture indicators, detects data transfers exceeding agreed scope, and flags processors with overdue security assessments — providing continuous evidence that processor oversight is active, not just contractual.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Network_Traffic data model, proxy/firewall logs, `gdpr_processor_register.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnotnull(processor_name)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_since_audit** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **audit_status** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **bytes_gb** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: audit_status, bytes_gb, days_since_audit, dest, dpa_signed, processor_name, sources, sub_processors.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (processor compliance status), Bar chart (data transfer by processor), Single value (overdue audits count), Timeline (transfer volume trends).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of third-party processor compliance monitoring — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
